Page 4 (Introduction, end of Abstract/Contributions discussion) and page 20 (Remark 2 following Theorem 1 in Section 3).

Because our problems involves both unknown linear parameter \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise distribution \(F\), we conjecture the obtained \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) rate is close to the optimal rate.

However, their constructed instances do not fit into our considered pricing problem with an additional linear structure \(x^\top\theta_0\). The additional unknown \(\theta_0\) makes the lower bound derivation harder and we leave it for future work.
title: Determine minimax regret rate for contextual dynamic pricing with unknown noise distribution
background_latex:
A seller repeatedly interacts with customers over periods \(t=1,\dots,T\). At time \(t\), a covariate (context) \(x_t\in\mathcal X\subset\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is observed, with \(\|x_t\|_\infty\le 1\). The customer's valuation is
\[
v_t = x_t^\top \theta_0 + z_t,
\]
where \(\theta_0\in\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is an unknown parameter (often assumed to satisfy \(\|\theta_0\|_1\le W\) for a known constant \(W\)), and \(\{z_t\}\) are i.i.d. draws from an unknown distribution with CDF \(F\). After the seller posts a price \(p_t>0\), the seller observes only the binary purchase indicator
\[
y_t = \mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}.
\]
The expected revenue from posting price \(p\) in context \(x\) is
\[
R(p;x) = p\,\mathbb P(v\ge p\mid x)= p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
Define the context-dependent optimal price
\[
p^*(x)\in \arg\max_{p>0} p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
The (pseudo-)regret over horizon \(T\) for a policy producing prices \(p_t\) is
\[
\mathcal R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T \Bigl( p^*(x_t)\bigl(1-F(p^*(x_t)-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) - p_t\bigl(1-F(p_t-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) \Bigr),
\]
and performance is measured by \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]\) over the randomness in the data and any policy randomization. The model is "distribution-free" in the sense that \(F\) is unknown and not restricted to a known parametric/log-concave family; the paper assumes regularity such as Lipschitz continuity of \(F\) in some analyses.
problem_definition_latex: Characterize the minimax-optimal regret rate in \(T\) for contextual dynamic pricing under the linear valuation model \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), given only binary purchase feedback \(y_t=\mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}\). In particular, determine whether there exists a policy with expected cumulative regret \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) (up to logarithmic factors), or prove a matching lower bound (up to logs) showing \(\inf_{\pi}\sup_{\theta_0,F}\mathbb E_\pi[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) (or the correct exponent), over an appropriate class of admissible \(F\) consistent with the paper's assumptions.
latex:
A seller repeatedly interacts with customers over periods \(t=1,\dots,T\). At time \(t\), a covariate (context) \(x_t\in\mathcal X\subset\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is observed, with \(\|x_t\|_\infty\le 1\). The customer's valuation is
\[
v_t = x_t^\top \theta_0 + z_t,
\]
where \(\theta_0\in\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is an unknown parameter (often assumed to satisfy \(\|\theta_0\|_1\le W\) for a known constant \(W\)), and \(\{z_t\}\) are i.i.d. draws from an unknown distribution with CDF \(F\). After the seller posts a price \(p_t>0\), the seller observes only the binary purchase indicator
\[
y_t = \mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}.
\]
The expected revenue from posting price \(p\) in context \(x\) is
\[
R(p;x) = p\,\mathbb P(v\ge p\mid x)= p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
Define the context-dependent optimal price
\[
p^*(x)\in \arg\max_{p>0} p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
The (pseudo-)regret over horizon \(T\) for a policy producing prices \(p_t\) is
\[
\mathcal R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T \Bigl( p^*(x_t)\bigl(1-F(p^*(x_t)-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) - p_t\bigl(1-F(p_t-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) \Bigr),
\]
and performance is measured by \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]\) over the randomness in the data and any policy randomization. The model is "distribution-free" in the sense that \(F\) is unknown and not restricted to a known parametric/log-concave family; the paper assumes regularity such as Lipschitz continuity of \(F\) in some analyses.

Characterize the minimax-optimal regret rate in \(T\) for contextual dynamic pricing under the linear valuation model \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), given only binary purchase feedback \(y_t=\mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}\). In particular, determine whether there exists a policy with expected cumulative regret \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) (up to logarithmic factors), or prove a matching lower bound (up to logs) showing \(\inf_{\pi}\sup_{\theta_0,F}\mathbb E_\pi[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) (or the correct exponent), over an appropriate class of admissible \(F\) consistent with the paper's assumptions.
location_in_paper: Page 4 (Introduction, end of Abstract/Contributions discussion) and page 20 (Remark 2 following Theorem 1 in Section 3).
open_problem_quote:
Because our problems involves both unknown linear parameter \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise distribution \(F\), we conjecture the obtained \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) rate is close to the optimal rate.

However, their constructed instances do not fit into our considered pricing problem with an additional linear structure \(x^\top\theta_0\). The additional unknown \(\theta_0\) makes the lower bound derivation harder and we leave it for future work.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question is a minimax-rate characterization rather than a concrete finite statement, but automated search can explore hard instance families (choices of \(F\), context sequences, and \(\theta_0\)) to empirically stress-test candidate algorithms and suggest lower-bound constructions. Verifying regret for a fixed policy/instance is simulable, but proving minimax optimality is not directly decidable by search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate policies and hard-instance generators can be represented programmatically, with fitness signals given by simulated regret over horizons across sampled or adversarially generated \((\theta_0,F)\) and contexts. This could help discover improved algorithms or plausible lower-bound instance patterns, though translating findings into rigorous minimax proofs would remain substantial theoretical work.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Luo–Sun–Liu, 2023) framed contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\) as a distribution-free problem under binary feedback \(y_t=\mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}\). Its DIP policy uses episodic estimation of \(\theta_0\) via a classification reduction and then reduces within-episode pricing to a perturbed linear bandit over a discretization of the residual \(p-x_t^\top\hat\theta\). Under Lipschitz \(F\) and a local quadratic revenue geometry (Assumptions 1–2), DIP yields a leading \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) term plus an additional term scaling with \(\sum_k \ell_k\,\mathbb E\|\hat\theta_{k-1}-\theta_0\|_1\), leaving open whether \(\tilde\Theta(T^{2/3})\) is the minimax rate when both \(\theta_0\) and \(F\) are unknown.

Subsequent work has essentially settled the minimax exponent under the natural “distribution-free but regular” class where \(F\) (equivalently demand \(1-F\)) is Lipschitz. In particular, "Minimax optimality in contextual dynamic pricing with general valuation models" proves a \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) regret upper bound for broad valuation classes (specializing to \(\tilde O(d_0^{1/3}T^{2/3})\) for linear valuations) and pairs it with a matching \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound (up to logarithmic factors), thereby establishing minimax optimality in \(T\) for Lipschitz \(F\). Complementarily, "Improved algorithms for contextual dynamic pricing" provides an explicit algorithmic route (VAPE) achieving \(\tilde O(d^{2/3}T^{2/3})\) regret under adversarial contexts and Lipschitz \(F\), and points to the \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound (e.g., via Xu–Wang-style constructions) showing the rate is unimprovable in this regime.

On the lower-bound side, "Towards agnostic feature-based dynamic pricing: Linear policies vs linear valuation with unknown noise" supplies a clean \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) minimax lower bound for the linear-valuation, unknown-noise, binary-feedback model, even under additional benign assumptions, clarifying that the \(2/3\) exponent is information-theoretic rather than an artifact of particular algorithms. Other partial-progress papers chart the landscape of assumptions: Explore-then-UCB achieves \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) only with extra second-order curvature, while smoothness-based semiparametric methods obtain rates depending on differentiability of \(F\). Finally, results such as "Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand" indicate that \(T^{2/3}\) can remain the right rate even without Lipschitzness when one assumes stochastic, well-conditioned contexts and bounded noise, suggesting robustness of the \(2/3\) barrier beyond the original Lipschitz class.

Overall, for the core setting emphasized by the source paper—linear valuations, adversarial contexts, binary feedback, and unknown Lipschitz noise CDF—the minimax regret rate is now characterized (up to logarithmic factors) as \(\tilde\Theta(T^{2/3})\), with "Minimax optimality in contextual dynamic pricing with general valuation models" providing the definitive minimax statement.

sources.title: Minimax optimality in contextual dynamic pricing with general valuation models
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives matching (up to logarithmic factors) minimax upper and lower bounds for distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing with binary feedback when the noise CDF is only assumed Lipschitz and valuations are bounded. In particular, under realizability and boundedness and Lipschitz continuity of the unknown noise CDF \(F\), Algorithm 1 achieves regret \(\mathrm{Reg}(T)=\tilde O\big(\rho_V(\delta)^{1/3} T^{2/3}\big)\) with high probability (Theorem 1), where \(\rho_V(\delta)\) is the statistical complexity of the valuation class via an offline regression oracle. For linear valuations \(v^*(x)=x^\top\theta_0\) in \(\mathbb R^{d_0}\), this yields \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{Reg}(T)]=\tilde O(d_0^{1/3}T^{2/3})\) (Corollary 1). They also cite/extend known lower bounds showing \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) minimax regret in the Lipschitz, non-contextual case (and provide a general \(\Omega(T^{(m+1)/(2m+1)})\) lower bound for \(m\)-times differentiable \(F\); Theorem 2), thereby establishing \(T^{2/3}\) minimax optimality (up to logs) under Lipschitz \(F\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to characterize the minimax regret rate for contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), given only binary purchase feedback, and in particular whether \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) is achievable and/or necessary. This paper directly targets that setting (and more general valuation classes): it proposes a distribution-free explore-then-UCB algorithm that does not require knowing the Lipschitz constant and proves a \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) regret upper bound (Theorem 1). For linear valuations it specializes to \(\tilde O(d_0^{1/3}T^{2/3})\) (Corollary 1). On the lower-bound side, it combines the known \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound for Lipschitz \(F\) in the non-contextual case (Xu and Wang 2022) with its upper bound to claim minimax optimality in \(T\) (up to logs) under Lipschitz noise; thus it resolves the exponent question posed in the open problem (the correct \(T\)-rate is \(T^{2/3}\) under Lipschitz \(F\)).

sources.title: Improved algorithms for contextual dynamic pricing
sources.theorem_or_result:
For contextual dynamic pricing with binary feedback and linear valuations with additive i.i.d. noise, the paper gives an algorithm (VAPE for Linear Valuations, Algorithm 2) that, under bounded contexts \(\|x_t\|\le B_x\), bounded parameter \(\|\theta\|\le B_\theta\), bounded noise \(|\xi_t|\le B_\xi\) and Lipschitz noise CDF \(F\) (equivalently Lipschitz demand \(D=1-F\)), achieves with probability \(1-T^{-1}\) regret
- \(R_T \le C\, d^{2/3} T^{2/3} \log(T)^{2/3}\)
for a constant \(C\) polynomial in \(B_\xi,B_x,B_\theta,L_\xi\) (Theorem 1). The paper also states this matches the known lower bound \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) for this setting (from Xu & Wang 2022).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the minimax regret rate in distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), under binary purchase feedback, and in particular whether \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) is achievable and/or whether a matching \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound holds over an admissible class of \(F\) (e.g., Lipschitz). This paper directly addresses that question in the linear valuation model \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta+\xi_t\) with unknown \(\theta\) and unknown (only Lipschitz) \(F\): it provides an explicit policy with \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) regret (Theorem 1) under adversarial contexts, and it explicitly points to an existing \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound for Lipschitz demand/noise (Xu & Wang 2022), concluding minimax optimality (up to logs and dimension factors). Thus, for the natural Lipschitz-CDF distribution-free class highlighted in the open problem, it essentially settles the minimax exponent at \(2/3\).

sources.title: Towards agnostic feature-based dynamic pricing: Linear policies vs linear valuation with unknown noise
sources.theorem_or_result: For contextual dynamic pricing with linear noisy valuations (LV) where valuations satisfy \(y_t=x_t^\top\theta^*+N_t\) with \(\|x_t\|_2\le B\), \(\|\theta^*\|_2\le 1\), and i.i.d. bounded noise \(N_t\in[-1,1]\) from an unknown distribution, the paper proposes algorithm D2-EXP4 achieving expected regret \(\tilde O(T^{3/4}+d^{1/2}T^{5/8})\) (Theorem 6). It also proves a minimax lower bound \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) for LV (Corollary 9), even under stronger conditions such as stochastic contexts, Lipschitz valuation/noise distribution, and unimodal revenue curve (via Theorem 7).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks to characterize the minimax regret rate for distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\) under binary purchase feedback, and in particular whether \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) is achievable or whether a matching \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound holds.
- This paper directly studies essentially the same LV model (linear valuation plus i.i.d. unknown noise, binary feedback) in an adversarial-context setting, and provides a \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound, supporting \(T^{2/3}\) as an information-theoretic barrier from below.
- However, its best upper bound for the LV model is \(\tilde O(T^{3/4}+d^{1/2}T^{5/8})\), not \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\). Thus it does not close the minimax rate; it narrows the gap by improving the lower bound to \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) and giving a sublinear algorithm without distributional assumptions beyond bounded noise.
- The paper also treats a different benchmark model (LP) where one competes with the best linear pricing policy and proves tight \(\tilde\Theta(d^{1/3}T^{2/3})\) regret; this is related but not the same as the LV minimax question in the open problem.

sources.title: Contextual dynamic pricing with unknown noise: Explore-then-ucb strategy and improved regrets
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proposes an Explore-then-UCB (ExUCB) policy for contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and unknown noise CDF \(F\). Under Assumptions 1–5 (bounded \(\|x_t\|_\infty\le 1\), \(\|\theta_0\|_1\le W\), bounded valuations, \(F\) Lipschitz, and a local quadratic/2nd-order smoothness condition on the revenue around \(p^*(x)\)), Theorem 1 proves \(\mathbb E[R_T]=\tilde O(d_0^2 T^{2/3})\). Under only Lipschitz \(F\) (Assumptions 1–4), Theorem 2 proves \(\mathbb E[R_T]=\tilde O(d_0 T^{3/4})\). For lower bounds under Assumptions 1–5, Theorem 3 shows that for any \(\delta>0\), no policy can achieve \(O(T^{3/5-\delta})\) regret (i.e., minimax regret is \(\tilde\Omega(T^{3/5})\) up to logs).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks to characterize the minimax-optimal regret rate for distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown \(F\) from binary feedback, and in particular whether \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) is achievable and/or whether a matching \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound holds over an admissible class of \(F\).
- Achievability: This paper gives the first \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) upper bound, but only under an additional 2nd-order smoothness (local quadratic curvature) assumption (Assumption 5) beyond Lipschitz continuity. Under Lipschitz-only, it attains \(\tilde O(T^{3/4})\), not \(T^{2/3}\).
- Lower bounds/minimax: It provides a new lower bound \(\tilde\Omega(T^{3/5})\) under Lipschitz+2nd-order smoothness, leaving a gap to the \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) upper bound; thus it does not establish minimax optimality nor a matching \(T^{2/3}\) lower bound in that class. The paper also notes prior \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bounds (Xu & Wang 2022) apply to Lipschitz-only but not to the 2nd-order smoothness class.
Therefore, it makes substantial partial progress: it achieves the conjectured \(T^{2/3}\) rate under stronger regularity and advances lower bounds in that smoother class, but does not fully resolve the minimax rate in the general distribution-free setting posed in the open problem.

sources.title: Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand
sources.theorem_or_result:
Theorem 1 (Xu–Wang 2026): Under (i) linear valuations 
\(y_t=\langle x_t,\theta^\star\rangle+\xi_t\), (ii) i.i.d. bounded-support noise \(\xi_t\in[-c,c]\) with unknown distribution (allowing arbitrary atoms/jumps; no Lipschitz/smoothness), (iii) i.i.d. stochastic contexts with \(\lambda_{\min}(\mathbb E[x_tx_t^\top])\ge \lambda_0>0\), and (iv) an interior “price buffer” condition, Algorithm 1 (CMRUP) achieves expected cumulative pseudo-regret \(\mathbb E[R_T]\le \tilde O(T^{2/3})\), with polylog\((T)\) and polynomial dependence on problem parameters hidden in \(\tilde O(\cdot)\).
sources.connection: Directly addresses the open problem of minimax regret rates for distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing with binary purchase feedback and unknown noise CDF. The paper provides an \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) upper bound even when the induced demand/survival function is non-Lipschitz and discontinuous (atoms/jumps), improving prior agnostic contextual bounds (\(\tilde O(T^{3/4})\)). It also argues tightness in \(T\) by embedding the classical non-contextual lower bound \(\Omega(T^{2/3})\) (Kleinberg–Leighton 2003) into its stochastic-context model (Appendix C.9), yielding a matching minimax lower bound in that restricted setting. However, it does not fully resolve the original open problem in the most general formulation because its main theorem assumes i.i.d. well-conditioned contexts (not adversarial contexts) and bounded-support noise plus an interior price-buffer condition; thus it characterizes the minimax rate only for that admissible subclass rather than all context sequences/classes of \(F\) contemplated in the original paper.

sources.title: Policy optimization using semiparametric models for dynamic pricing
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes an episodic explore-then-exploit policy for contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and unknown noise CDF \(F\) (binary purchase feedback). Under regularity/identifiability conditions (notably Assumption 2.1 ensuring a unique optimal price and smoothness of \(F\)), it proves regret upper bounds:
- If \(F\in C^{(m)}\) with \(m\ge 2\), then \(\mathrm{Regret}(T)\le \tilde O\big((Td)^{\frac{2m+1}{4m-1}}\big)\) (Theorem 4.1; logs and some \(d\)-dependence explicit in the statement).
- If \(F\) is “super-smooth” (Fourier transform decays exponentially; Assumption 4.7), then \(\mathrm{Regret}(T)\le \tilde O\big((Td)^{1/2}\big)\) (Theorem 4.3).
It also extends the \(C^{(m)}\) bound to \(\beta\)-mixing (dependent) contexts (Theorem 4.2).
sources.connection: Directly addresses the same distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing model with linear signal \(x_t^\top\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), with only binary purchase feedback. It provides explicit regret upper bounds as a function of the smoothness of \(F\): \(\tilde O(T^{(2m+1)/(4m-1)})\) (up to dimension/log factors) and \(\tilde O(\sqrt T)\) for super-smooth \(F\). This is progress toward characterizing minimax rates, but it does not establish matching minimax lower bounds for the nonparametric \(F\) class; the paper itself notes that tight lower bounds beyond the generic \(\Omega(\sqrt T)\) parametric-style lower bound remain open for finite \(m\). In particular, it does not prove a \(\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) lower bound nor show \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) is minimax-optimal; instead it achieves exponents \(\frac{2m+1}{4m-1}\) (e.g. \(5/7\) for \(m=2\)) which are generally different from \(2/3\).
